data.content:
Now I have the full picture. Let me create the `Directory.Packages.props` and update all project files.

Conflicts found:
- `System.Text.Json`: 10.0.1 vs 8.0.4 → **10.0.1**
- `Azure.Identity`: 1.13.2 vs 1.11.4 → **1.13.2**

arguments.file_text:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageVersion Include="Azure.Identity" Version="1.13.2" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting" Version="1.15.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="10.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.9.3" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
Now I understand the structure. Let me identify all packages and their versions across all projects:

| Package | Api | Shared | Worker | Tests | Highest |
|---------|-----|--------|--------|-------|---------|
| System.Text.Json | 10.0.1 | 10.0.1 | 8.0.4 | 10.0.1 | 10.0.1 |
| Azure.Identity | 1.13.2 | 1.11.4 | - | - | 1.13.2 |
| Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting | - | - | 8.0.1 | - | 8.0.1 |


| OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting | - | - | 1.15.0 | - | 1.15.0 |
| xunit | - | - | - | 2.9.3 | 2.9.3 |

Now I need to set up centralized package management by creating a Directory.Packages.props file at the solution root that enables version management centrally and lists all the highest versions, then strip out the version attributes from each project's package references.

</Project>

data.content:
Done. Here's what I did:

**Created** `Directory.Packages.props` with 5 centralized package versions.

**Resolved 2 version conflicts** (picked highest):
- `System.Text.Json`: 8.0.4 vs 10.0.1 → **10.0.1**
- `Azure.Identity`: 1.11.4 vs 1.13.2 → **1.13.2**

**Updated 4 `.csproj` files** — removed all `Version` attributes from `<PackageReference>` elements so versions are managed centrally.
